summaries.History: The Channel Islands, including Guernsey, Herm, Sark, and Jethou, have a prehistoric history tied to sea-going peoples who were drawn to the islands as a constant visual presence from the north-west coast of Jersey and the Cotentin peninsula, with palaeogeographic models tracing the region's development from 9000 BC to 4000 BC, during which eustatic sea-level rise significantly altered the landscape. Archaeological evidence from Guernsey includes funerary monuments, menhirs, polished stone rings likely made of schist, Neolithic and Bronze Age pottery, axe finds, stone rings, and artefacts of flint and stone including scrapers, transverse arrowheads, and cores made from local beach pebble flint. The geology of Guernsey is documented alongside topographic data at mean sea-level, and the broader prehistoric context involves analysis of sea-going vessels and the movement of peoples across the region. Jethou's recorded history includes a notable maritime incident in which a vessel returning from a Spanish port carrying cork and hemp ran upon the island during a dense fog, and the island's prominent granite feature La Fauconnaire, nearly two hundred feet high and surmounted by a fifteen-foot white stone beacon, is joined to the mainland by a causeway of rocks at low water, referencing a narrative of island life connected to the Channel Isles more broadly described in a work evoking the Crusoe tradition.
summaries.Governance: Guernsey is signed up to the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands of International Importance and has 2 designated Ramsar sites: Lihou Island and L'Erée Headland, and Herm, Jethou and The Humps. Guernsey's first Biodiversity Strategy was adopted by the States of Guernsey in 2014. Herm, Jethou and The Humps were designated under this framework, with Sites of Special Significance (SSS) affording protection within the Island Development Plan in recognition of their archaeological, botanical, geological, scientific, cultural, zoological or other qualities. The State of Nature report was produced to provide information on the state of habitats and species on the islands of Guernsey, Lihou, Herm, Jethou and The Humps, intended to guide conservation efforts by government, industry, the third sector, businesses, and the general public, with actions categorized as government actions, NGO actions, national/international actions, and collaborative actions. The Guernsey Conservation Herd and Guernsey Seasearch were both established under La Société Guernesiaise, and the UK State of Nature 2013 report was published as part of the broader national/international governance context influencing local conservation policy.
summaries.Wildlife: The Herm, Jethou and The Humps Ramsar site was designated in 2015 as a wetland of international importance spanning 1,850 hectares of shallow marine ecosystems, including surrounding reefs, shipwrecks, and seabeds, with an exceptionally large tidal range of up to 10 metres. The area supports diverse habitats such as eelgrass (Zostera) beds and maerl gravel, serving as spawning and nursery grounds for fish like European sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax) and Atlantic mackerel (Scomber scombrus). Globally threatened species found in the area include the green ormer (Haliotis tuberculata), Balearic shearwater (Puffinus mauretanicus), and marine mammals such as Atlantic grey seals and basking sharks, as well as Black Sea bream and nine species of bird including puffins. Wildlife and puffin-spotting trips in the area can be undertaken with Island Rib Voyages or Outdoor Guernsey, and further wildlife information for Herm is available at herm.com. The Gouliot Headland on the west coast of Sark is also noted as a wildlife-relevant location.
summaries.Jethou in popular culture: No relevant information regarding Jethou in popular culture is available.
